does: This paper introduces a framework that converts organizational policy documents and design artifacts into executable runtime guardrails for AI agents. The core method, called Policy-as-Prompt, parses unstructured documents like product requirements and technical design documents to construct a source-linked policy tree, which is then compiled into lightweight prompt-based classifiers that monitor agent behavior in real time. The system enforces principles of least privilege and data minimization while maintaining complete provenance tracking. Evaluation demonstrates the approach reduces prompt-injection vulnerabilities, blocks out-of-scope requests, limits harmful outputs, and generates auditable decision rationales. The framework integrates human-in-the-loop review and provides compliance documentation aligned with governance standards.
connects: This work bridges policy and AI safety by extending the policy-as-code paradigm from traditional software governance to large language model agents. It builds on prior research in runtime monitoring, security guardrails for AI systems, and prompt-based control mechanisms. The approach connects to existing work on regulatory machine learning and compliance automation, while also drawing from formal methods for policy specification and verification. It responds to increasing organizational need for auditable AI systems in regulated domains, addressing limitations of purely intent-based safety approaches by making governance requirements machine-enforceable. The focus on traceability and provenance relates to growing work on interpretability and accountability in AI systems.
enables: This framework enables several future directions in AI governance and safety. Organizations can now deploy agents in regulated and safety-critical settings with verifiable compliance guarantees backed by auditable decision logs. The approach points toward continuous runtime compliance monitoring that adapts as policies evolve, reducing the gap between policy intent and implementation. It opens possibilities for standardized, machine-readable policy formats across organizations and regulatory bodies. The method could scale to handle complex policy interactions and enable formal verification of policy compliance properties. Future work might extend this to distributed agent systems, multi-stakeholder governance scenarios, and integration with broader AI risk management frameworks, potentially supporting real-time policy updates without retraining underlying models.
